content.problemStatement:
In this worked-out example, you’ll learn how to restrict access to specific sections of your website using custom middleware. 
We are building a site where:
- Anyone can visit the homepage.
- Users can register and log in.
- Only logged-in users should be allowed to access the Members Area (/members/).
- If an unauthenticated user tries to access /members/, they should be redirected to an Access Denied page.

We will implement this restriction using custom middleware, which acts as a gatekeeper before any view is called.

**The project includes:**
- A homepage with links to REGISTER, LOGIN, and GOTO MEMBERS AREA.
- Register and login views using Django’s built-in forms.
- A members/ view that should be protected.
- An access_denied.html template to show unauthorized access along with other necessary templates at /myapp/templates.

**Project flow**:
- Users can register at /register/ and log in at /login/.
- The homepage is open to everyone at /.
- `/members/` should be restricted to authenticated users only.
- We write a custom middleware:
   - Check `/myapp/middleware.py` file where we created a `MembersOnlyMiddleware` class.
   - If a user tries to access any path starting with `/members/` and is not logged in, redirect them to `/access-denied/`.
   - Otherwise, allow the request to continue normally.

- Register the middleware:
   - We added the custom middleware to the `MIDDLEWARE` list in `settings.py`
   - Make sure it's below Django’s `authentication` middleware so request.user is available.

Click on **RUN** which will make migrations and start the server.
- Open the browser on a new tab.
- Try clicking on `Go to Members Area` without logging in — you should see the Access Denied page.
- Register a new user, log in, then go to /members/ — you should be able to access it.
